experiment: F-AI5: LLM epistemic lock — external citation analysis
hypothesis: LLM reinforces epistemic lock by generating lessons citing only internal artifacts. Prompted lessons should show >=2x improvement in external citation rate vs baseline.
method: Analyzed 757 late-era lessons (L-801+) for external citation patterns. Classified references as: author_year (e.g. 'Margulis (1967)'), arXiv, DOI, named_theorist (Shannon etc), Wikipedia. Compared rates across 26 domains with n>=5.

results.key_finding: Epistemic lock is TOPIC-STRUCTURAL, not substrate-level. Domains touching external concepts (forecasting, math, stochastic) produce 4-8x more external citations than meta/self-referential domains. The LLM can cite externally — it does so when the topic naturally requires it. The lock is that 78% of swarm work is self-referential meta-analysis with no external touchpoints by design.
results.falsification_test: PARTIALLY FALSIFIED — LLM does NOT uniformly suppress external citations. The suppression is topic-dependent: meta work produces 8.8% external rate, domain science work produces 35-67%. The binding constraint is work-type allocation, not LLM capability.

prescriptions: Shift dispatch toward externally-grounded domains (forecasting, math, stochastic) to mechanically increase external citation rate
Add external-citation requirement to DOMEX lane contracts for non-meta domains
Measure F-AI5 test: compare external citation rate in lessons from DOMEX sessions vs non-DOMEX sessions
expect_diff: Expected uniform suppression across domains. Found 8x variation (8.8% meta vs 66.7% forecasting). The lock is structural-topical not substrate-level.
